text: Laparoscopic ultra-low anterior resection with intracorporeal coloanal stapled anastomosis for low rectal cancer. Do we always need robotic surgery or transanal total mesorectal excision to achieve a good oncological and sphincter sparing dissection? A seventy-one-year-old female with performance status zero presented with rectal bleeding. Colonoscopy revealed a sessile polyp four centimeters from the anal margin. Subsequent MRI scan was reported as T-III tumor with possible involvement of external sphincter, but no evidence of extramural invasion and no involved lymph nodes. CT scan was negative for metastatic disease. Interestingly, initial endoscopic biopsies showed high-grade dysplasia only. The patient then had endoscopic ultrasound with repeat biopsy, which confirmed a diagnosis of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma involving lateral wall but not extending to levator muscles or external sphincter. The patient completed long-course chemoradiotherapy as per recommendation from the multidisciplinary team. Repeat imaging confirmed good response to treatment with no convincing residual tumour on MRI and no metastatic disease on the CT scan.

The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum and identifies the inferior mesenteric vessels. The inferior mesenteric vein is dissected and divided near the ligament of Treitz, creating a subvenous window. Dissection continues caudally to reveal the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery, which is then divided close to its origin from the abdominal aorta.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic ultra-low anterior resection for rectal cancer located between three and five centimeters from the anal verge. The dissection proceeds in a craniocaudal direction, opening the mesorectal plane and mobilizing the rectum. The intersphincteric plane is followed to reach the dentate line using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques.

The surgeon palpates the tumor per rectum and marks it laparoscopically with a clip. Laparoscopic dissection proceeds in the intersphincteric plane, allowing division of the anal canal at or just above the dentate line. A flexible endostapler is inserted into the intersphincteric plane to reach the anal canal, ensuring adequate margins for resection. The stapler is closed and held in position to compress the tissues for ninety seconds before firing, enhancing compression and seal on the thicker tissues of the anal canal.

The pelvic floor is visible after the division of the anal canal. The descending colon and splenic flexure have been mobilized, and the specimen is delivered through a mini fan and steel incision approximately four to five centimeters long, created using a muscle-splitting technique. The descending colon is divided with satisfactory perfusion of the remaining proximal colon. The anvil of the circular stapler is secured at the proximal end of the bowel, and the circular stapler is inserted transanally. The staple spike is delivered slowly near the midpoint of the staple line, with laparoscopic right angle instruments used to minimize the risk of tearing the staple line due to the thicker and denser tissues near the anal margin.

The surgeon secures the anvil to the spike of the circular stapler and closes it under direct vision, ensuring there is no tension on the anastomosis and checking the colon's orientation to prevent twisting. The stapler is held closed for ninety seconds to achieve optimal tissue compression before firing. After firing, the stapler is twisted to dislodge it from the anastomosis and removed. A closed suction Blake drain is placed below the pelvic brim in the retroanastomotic space, and a temporary diverting ileostomy is created in the right lower quadrant.

This case illustrates that in experienced hands laparoscopic approach to ultra-low anterior resection is feasible and allows intracorporeal ultra-low color anal anastomosis at the level of dentate line with total mesorectal excision. If laparoscopic expertise is available, this top-down laparoscopic approach can be easier compared to intersphincteric dissection via perineal approach and is certainly more cost-effective than a robotic approach. It is also minimally invasive and avoids the need for a permanent stoma or a perineal wound, decreasing the risk of infections and enhancing the recovery process. Histology from the surgical specimen revealed residual small islands of viable moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma invading into muscularis propria with clear surgical margins and absent lymphovascular invasion. Following uncomplicated post-op recovery, patient was discharged home on day eight. There were no complications identified on
